chemicals.concentration_notes: While these allergenic lactones are present in all parts of the tulip plant, they are most heavily and dangerously concentrated in the bulbs
chemicals.description: in cats: The provided sources do not detail the specific cellular mechanism of action for these compounds. However, clinically, they act as severe irritants and allergens. When ingested, they cause intense irritation to the gastrointestinal tract, leading to profuse drooling (ptyalism), nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and depression1.... If a cat consumes a large amount, the toxins can cause systemic
chemicals.name: Allergenic lactones, specifically identified as tulipalin A and tulipalin B

chemicals.chemical_formula: Not provided in the given sources.
chemicals.concentration_notes: Like the lactones, these toxic alkaloids are heavily concentrated in the tulip bulbs
chemicals.description: in cats: Similar to the allergenic lactones, the sources do not provide the internal biological mechanism of action for these alkaloids, but note that they contribute to the plant's overall toxicity. They cause significant stomach upset, oral irritation, and in severe cases (especially if the bulb is ingested), they can cause convulsions, tremors, and cardiac arrhythmias
chemicals.name: Alkaloids

description: Tulip (Tulipa spp.) is a popular spring bulb flower that is toxic to cats throughout all its parts, with the highest concentration of toxins in the bulbs. It contains tulipalin A and tulipalin B — allergenic lactone compounds — and other irritant glycosides. The bulb is particularly dangerous as it is accessible during planting and storage seasons.
name: Tulip
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: tulipalin A and tulipalin B (allergenic lactone compounds), along with irritant glycosides; bulbs most concentrated
Mechanism: tulipalins cause direct mucosal irritation and allergic contact dermatitis; large bulb ingestion may cause CNS depression and cardiac effects
Symptoms: vomiting, drooling, diarrhea, lethargy; large bulb ingestion may cause tremors, abnormal heart rate, or breathing difficulty
Bulbs are especially dangerous during autumn planting or spring storage — keep stored bulbs away from cats
Contact your vet if your cat ingests a bulb or shows more than mild GI symptoms

symptoms.name: Irritation to the mouth, throat, and skin, leading to profuse drooling (ptyalism)
symptoms.notes: This is typically a primary reaction to the allergenic lactones found in the plant12. 2. Vomiting and Diarrhea

symptoms.name: Vomiting, diarrhea, and significant stomach upset
symptoms.notes: These are the most common digestive symptoms3. Furthermore, if a cat ingests large, intact pieces of the tulip bulb, it can cause a physical intestinal obstruction8. 3. Depression

symptoms.name: Depression
symptoms.notes: This generalized symptom of toxicosis is noted alongside severe stomach upset68. 4. Elevated Heart Rate and Arrhythmias

symptoms.name: Elevated heart rate (tachycardia) and cardiac arrhythmias
symptoms.notes: Cardiovascular abnormalities typically present only after a cat has ingested larger amounts of the plant, especially the highly toxic bulb where the dangerous compounds are concentrated1.... 5. Respiratory Distress

symptoms.name: Changes to respiration and difficulty breathing
symptoms.notes: Similar to cardiac symptoms, respiratory distress is a severe systemic sign that occurs following a larger ingestion of the plant or its bulb1.... 6. Tremors and Convulsions

symptoms.name: Tremors and convulsions (seizures)
symptoms.notes: These severe neurological symptoms manifest when significant amounts of the plant, particularly the bulb containing the highest concentration of toxic alkaloids and lactones, are consumed

treatments.description: If the ingestion was recent, the veterinarian may medically induce vomiting to empty the stomach of the plant material. This is often followed by administering activated charcoal to bind any remaining toxins in the gastrointestinal tract, preventing them from being absorbed into the bloodstream
treatments.name: Emesis Induction and Activated Charcoal
treatments.notes: This step is crucial for eliminating the toxic allergenic lactones and alkaloids before they can be absorbed, especially if the cat has eaten pieces of the highly concentrated tulip bulb1. You should never attempt to induce vomiting at home without professional veterinary advice, as you risk injurin

treatments.description: The veterinary team will administer IV fluids to rehydrate the cat, help eliminate toxins, and support overall organ function
treatments.name: Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy
treatments.notes: This intervention is highly critical for tulip poisoning. The plant triggers profuse drooling, vomiting, and diarrhea, which quickly lead to severe fluid loss and dehydration1.... 3. Cardiovascular and Respiratory Support (If Severe)

treatments.description: The veterinarian will closely monitor the cat's vital signs and administer specific medications or breathing support if cardiovascular or respiratory abnormalities develop
treatments.name: Cardiac and Respiratory Monitoring and Pharmacological Support
treatments.notes: While mild exposures generally only cause gastrointestinal upset, if a cat ingests larger amounts of the tulip—particularly the highly toxic bulb—it can lead to severe systemic signs such as an elevated heart rate (tachycardia), cardiac arrhythmias, and difficulty breathing, requiring targeted emerg

treatments.description: The veterinarian will closely monitor the cat and provide specific treatments to ease discomfort, which may include gastroprotectant medications to soothe the digestive tract
treatments.name: Symptomatic Treatment and Monitoring
treatments.notes: The allergenic lactones in tulips act as severe irritants to the tissues in the mouth and throat27. Supportive care ensures the cat remains comfortable while the toxins pass through its system. Additionally, veterinarians may need to monitor for potential intestinal blockages if large, intact pieces
